question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed one substantive didactic question in Turn 1 (asymptotic complexity + spotting the bug), then moved to implementation. Only one teachable moment was actively engaged, though it was the central one for this problem.

question_quality.rationale: The single question hit the crux: it juxtaposed O(n) vs O(√n) AND embedded a classic gotcha (incorrectly including n itself when i=1 in the divisor-pair approach). That's a high-quality two-for-one — algorithmic trade-off plus boundary-condition bug hunting.

scaffolding.rationale: The complexity question was well-paired with the bug spot, and no answers were spoiled. However, the Pair stopped questioning after Turn 1 — there was no follow-up scaffold (e.g., why iterate only a<b, or sieve approaches) before writing the final code.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is direct and warm ("Exactly right on both counts"). The Turn 3 detection of the user pasting an AI response was handled gracefully, and the offer to explore a sieve approach kept the door open for further learning.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code in euler21.py produces 31626, matching the baseline's correct answer. Implementation quality (O(√n), perfect-square guard) is equivalent to baseline.

friction.rationale: Experiment took 3 turns / 84s vs baseline's 1 turn / 22s, but the extra length delivered a genuine pedagogical payoff (complexity comparison + bug identification). The Turn 3 stray AI-paste added noise but the Pair handled it efficiently.
